{"query":"COP30 adaptation finance tripling 2035 target amount USD baseline year","model":"mako","response_time_ms":925,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.wri.org/insights/tripling-adaptation-finance-goal","title":"Reaching $120 Billion in International Adaptation Finance Is Possible — Here’s What It Takes | World Resources Institute","content":"As COP30 triples the adaptation finance goal to $120 billion, we explore what banks, governments and private finance must do to meet it by 2035.
The final outcome in the Belém package — the set of agreements and decisions adopted by countries at COP30 — calls for efforts to reach this new target as part of the broader $300 billion in climate finance (known as the new collective quantified goal or NCQG).
We explore here how the tripling of the adaptation finance goal could be achieved by 2035, the target year for the $300 billion NCQG.
However, global adaptation finance is still far from this amount, even though investments will pay off trillions of dollars in avoided losses, many lives saved, economic, environmental and social benefits and greater stability amid the shocks of a warming world.
At COP26 in 2021, developed countries agreed to at least double their adaptation finance for developing countries from 2019 levels of around $18.8 billion to approximately $40 billion.
Using this $390 billion number, if 50% were to go to climate action — close to current MDB targets — MDBs would provide around $195 billion in total climate finance by 2035.
However, tripling IDA will be challenging given the financial and political support required to make this happen.
We assume it starts to do so in 2028, sending bilateral adaptation finance upward again at around 3% to 4% per year (a bit over average inflation) — reaching $20 billion by 2035."},{"url":"https://www.carbonbrief.org/analysis-why-cop30s-tripling-adaptation-finance-target-is-less-ambitious-than-it-seems/","title":"Analysis: Why COP30’s ‘tripling adaptation finance’ target is less ambitious than it seems - Carbon Brief","content":"At COP26 in Glasgow in 2021, a target was agreed for developed nations to double the amount of adaptation finance they would provide to developing countries by 2025.
However, the text does allude to the “doubling” target agreed at COP26 in Glasgow, which some analysts say is an indicator of what the baseline should be.
The “doubling” target set at COP26 was based on adaptation finance “provided” by developed countries.
More MDB funding would also automatically be counted, as the new adaptation goal includes MDB funds that are attributable to developing countries, as set out in the NCQG.
When added together, UNEP concludes these needs amount to $365bn each year for developing countries between 2023 and 2035.
Even in a scenario based on the LDCs’ original proposal of tripling adaptation finance to $120bn by 2030, the UNEP report concluded that a “significant” gap would have remained."},{"url":"https://odi.org/en/insights/the-cop30-goal-to-triple-adaptation-finance-by-2035/","title":"The COP30 goal to 'triple adaptation finance by 2035' | ODI: Think change","content":"While a strong signal of ambition, the goal set at COP30 to triple adaptation finance by 2035 lacked clarity on key elements like the baseline year and sources of finance."},{"url":"https://news.un.org/en/story/2025/11/1166433","title":"Belém COP30 delivers climate finance boost and a pledge to plan fossil fuel transition | UN News","content":"New initiatives: Launch of the Global Implementation Accelerator and Belém Mission to 1.5°C to drive ambition and implementation.
More than 80 countries backed Brazil’s proposal for a formal ‘roadmap.’
After two weeks of intense negotiations, the adopted text calls for mobilizing at least $1.3 trillion per year by 2035 for climate action, alongside tripling adaptation finance and operationalizing the loss and damage fund agreed at COP28.
In the closing meeting, COP30 President André Corrêa do Lago acknowledged what was left out of the deal:"},{"url":"https://www.wri.org/technical-perspectives/cop30-progress-1-3-trillion","title":"Progress at COP30 toward $1.3 Trillion, and What's Next","date":"by M Caldwell · Cited by 1","content":"by M Caldwell · Cited by 1 — COP30 laid the groundwork for delivering $1.3 trillion in climate finance to developing countries by 2035. least triple this amount by 2035."}]}

After two weeks of intense negotiations, the text adopted at COP30 calls for mobilizing at least $1.3 trillion per year by 2035 for climate action, alongside tripling adaptation finance. It also launches two major initiatives to help countries deliver on their national climate action plans, and for the first time, acknowledges the need to tackle climate disinformation, pledging to promote information integrity. ### Related information. #### Facts and figures. #### Cutting emissions. #### Clean energy. #### Adapting to climate change. #### Financing climate action. #### Explainers. #### International cooperation. #### Resources.","score":0.8201387,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference","title":"2025 United Nations Climate Change Conference","content":"The **2025 United Nations Climate Change Conference** or **Conference of the Parties to the UNFCCC**, more commonly known as **COP30**, was the 30th session of the United Nations Climate Change Conference. | Date | 10–21 November 2025 (2025-11-10 – 2025-11-21) |. It was held at the Hangar Convention Centre\") [pt \"pt:Hangar (centro de convenções)\")] in Belém, Brazil, from 10 to 21 November 2025. The city's candidacy was announced in 2022 by Brazilian President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva during his visit to COP27, held in Sharm El Sheikh, Egypt, and was made official in January 2023. In January 2025, President Lula da Silva appointed Brazilian diplomat André Corrêa do Lago as president of COP30. The decision was welcomed by climate activists, given Lago's experience in climate negotiations and leadership in climate justice discussions. On 4 November 2025, President Lula da Silva signed a law temporarily symbolically transferring the Brazilian national capital from Brasília to Belém for the duration of COP30, from 11 to 21 November 2025.","score":0.27012825,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.climate.columbia.edu/cop30","title":"COP30 UN Climate Change Conference","content":"COP30, the 2025 United Nations Climate Change Conference, marks a pivotal opportunity to drive meaningful climate action on a global scale.Read more","score":0.23051286,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://unfccc.int/cop30","title":"UN Climate Change Conference - Belém, November 2025","content":"Nov 22, 2025 — COP30 showed that climate cooperation is alive and kicking, keeping humanity in the fight for a livable planet, with a firm resolve to keep 1.5C ...Read more","score":0.19064902,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://climateandhealthalliance.org/cop30","title":"COP30: Climate and Health at the UN Climate Change Conference - The Global Climate and Health Alliance","content":"Global Climate and Health Alliance. # COP30: Climate and Health at the UN Climate Change Conference. Find out more about the 2025 UN Climate Change Conference and what it meant for the climate and health movement. ## Find out more about the 2025 UN Climate Change Conference (COP 30) which took place November 10-21 2025 in Belém, Brazil, and what it meant for the climate and health movement. It was a test of whether the world can still work together to protect the most basic human right of all, the right to health. This was the 30th Climate COP to take place- and is referred to as ‘COP30’. Ahead of the COP 30 climate summit, the health community is calling on all governments to honor responsibilities to support adaptation and climate action in developing countries, and to lead the transition away from fossil fuels.","score":0.17171332,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.84,"request_id":"417fca38-8497-4a49-94ab-f784c8249626"}
